primary_diagnosis.name: Severe candidal (Candida) intertrigo of the groin and abdominal skin folds with maceration, painful fissuring and secondary infection risk, driven by recent broad-spectrum antibiotics, moisture and occlusion in an immobile bedbound patient

comorbidities.name: Recent broad-spectrum antibiotic therapy (for urinary tract infection)
Immobility / bedbound state with skin-fold occlusion and friction
Urinary incontinence with chronic moisture in the skin folds

pathophysiology_summary: Warm, occluded, chronically moist intertriginous skin (deep groin and infra-pannus folds) in a bedbound, incontinent patient provides an ideal environment for Candida albicans overgrowth. A recent broad-spectrum antibiotic course for a UTI has suppressed the competing commensal bacterial flora, allowing candidal proliferation. Yeast invasion of the macerated stratum corneum produces the classic beefy-red erythema, satellite papules and pustules extending beyond the advancing border, and a white curd-like exudate. Persistent moisture, friction and movement have caused maceration and deep linear fissuring at the base of the folds, which now bleed and are intensely painful. The denuded, fissured skin is a portal for secondary bacterial infection (staphylococcal/streptococcal), and in a frail patient this can progress to cellulitis or, rarely, necrotising soft-tissue infection and sepsis. At presentation the erythema is superficial without deep induration and the patient is afebrile with stable observations, so frank cellulitis/sepsis is not yet established — but it cannot be excluded on video and the threshold for in-person examination is low. Treatment targets the yeast (topical + systemic antifungal), calms inflammation short-term (low-potency topical steroid), and controls the moisture/occlusion that perpetuates the process.

differential.evidence_basis: Painful, raw, weeping, fissured erythema in occluded groin/abdominal folds of a bedbound, incontinent, recently-antibiotic-treated patient is the classic setting for candidal intertrigo.

differential.diagnosis: Bacterial cellulitis of the skin folds
differential.evidence_basis: Red, painful skin with fissures could represent, or be complicated by, bacterial cellulitis — cannot be excluded on video and would change urgency; must be actively screened for.
Urine and moisture cause irritant dermatitis in the same distribution, but do not produce curd-like exudate or satellite pustules.
Can present as well-demarcated flexural erythema, but the acute onset, curd-like exudate and satellite pustules point away from it.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Candidal intertrigo should lead early, but bacterial cellulitis must be explicitly held as the important-not-to-miss alternative that drives the in-person-exam decision.

differential.diagnosis: Candidal intertrigo with secondary infection risk
differential.evidence_basis: White curd-like exudate, satellite papulopustules beyond the border, recent broad-spectrum antibiotics and chronically moist occluded folds together are strongly characteristic.

differential.diagnosis: Bacterial cellulitis of the skin folds
differential.evidence_basis: Still cannot be excluded remotely; superficial erythema without deep induration and an afebrile patient make established cellulitis less likely, but the fissures are an entry portal.
A contributing factor but does not explain the pustules or curd-like exudate.

differential_progression.scoring_note: By this stage the antibiotic driver and the candidal signs should consolidate the leading diagnosis while cellulitis remains held open.

differential.diagnosis: Candidal intertrigo with maceration and fissuring, secondary infection not yet established
differential.evidence_basis: Red-flag screen negative: no rapidly spreading erythema, no necrosis, no deep induration, afebrile with stable observations — arguing against established cellulitis/necrotising infection now.

Screened negative at present (no necrosis, no pain-out-of-proportion beyond the fissures, no systemic toxicity) but is the catastrophic what-must-not-be-missed if the picture changes — hence the mandatory 000 escalation edge.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A negative red-flag screen supports same-day in-person management rather than emergency transfer, but the escalation triggers must be handed over explicitly.

differential.diagnosis: Severe candidal intertrigo with painful fissuring, secondary bacterial infection risk (antibiotic/moisture/occlusion driven)
differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed clinically by the classic constellation and setting; requires in-person examination to inspect fissure depth and exclude cellulitis, combined topical + oral antifungal, moisture control and wound care.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final answer is same-day in-person review + antifungal-based management, not emergency transfer and not self-care-only.

red_flags.description: Features of spreading bacterial cellulitis or necrotising soft-tissue infection — rapidly advancing erythema down the thighs, black/necrotic patches, or a hard indurated deep swelling
red_flags.clinical_significance: In a frail bedbound patient with fissured skin, secondary deep infection can be limb- and life-threatening; these features mandate emergency transfer.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Undetected necrotising infection or advancing cellulitis progresses to sepsis and death.

red_flags.description: Systemic sepsis from a secondary skin infection — new fever, rigors, tachycardia, hypotension, confusion or reduced responsiveness
red_flags.clinical_significance: Afebrile with stable observations now, but a recently-antibiotic-treated frail patient can deteriorate quickly; a low threshold for escalation is required.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Sepsis progresses unrecognised in a patient who cannot self-report deterioration.

red_flags.description: Severe pain from bleeding fissures preventing washing and repositioning in a bedbound, incontinent patient
red_flags.clinical_significance: Interruption of turning/hygiene creates immediate risk of pressure injury and a persistent infection portal, compounding the skin problem.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Pressure injury and worsening secondary infection develop while essential care is deferred.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Under-call as a simple nappy rash
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Dismissing this as a trivial moisture rash and recommending only barrier cream/self-care, missing that a frail bedbound patient with bleeding fissures on recent antibiotics needs an in-person examination to exclude secondary bacterial infection.

Escalating straight to emergency ambulance transfer when there is no systemic sepsis (afebrile, stable observations) and no necrotising features — this over-triages a same-day in-person problem and needlessly burdens a frail patient.

Reaching for more oral antibiotics because the skin looks infected — this would worsen the candidal overgrowth, whose driver is the recent broad-spectrum antibiotic course. Antibiotics are only warranted if genuine secondary bacterial cellulitis is confirmed on in-person examination.

Treating the burning inflammation with a topical corticosteroid alone would suppress inflammation while allowing the yeast to flourish (tinea/candida incognito); the steroid must be combined with an antifungal and used only short-term.

Attributing the refusal of care and distress to the patient's dependency rather than to a treatable painful infection, or attempting full remote management when the fissures require in-person inspection, wound care and prescription.

cannot_determine_via_chat: The depth of the fissures and whether there is deep tissue involvement
Whether there is deep induration or early bacterial cellulitis beneath the erythema
Whether an early necrotising soft-tissue infection is developing
Reliable, calibrated vital signs (ward readings are caregiver-obtained and unverified)
A skin swab / wound culture to confirm the organism and any secondary bacterial pathogen
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange a SAME-DAY in-person medical review (GP/locum home visit to the nursing home, or urgent care) to examine the skin folds, exclude secondary bacterial cellulitis, and prescribe combined topical antifungal + short-term low-potency steroid and oral antifungal with wound care; meanwhile keep the folds dry and hand over the emergency escalation triggers to nursing staff. Escalate to 000/ED immediately if necrotising or systemic-sepsis features appear.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Moderate — the surface appearance is characteristic of candidal intertrigo, but a secondary bacterial cellulitis cannot be excluded on video in a frail patient. The real risk is under-examining (managing remotely and missing deep infection) or over-triaging to an ambulance, not the primary diagnosis itself.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Australian guidance on intertrigo and cutaneous candidiasis (e.g. Therapeutic Guidelines: Dermatology — candidal intertrigo)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Topical antifungal (e.g. clotrimazole/nystatin) with moisture reduction is first-line; a short course of low-potency topical corticosteroid may relieve inflammation; oral antifungal (e.g. fluconazole) is reserved for extensive, severe or refractory disease; treat/avoid predisposing factors including broad-spectrum antibiotics and occlusion.
